audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Joshua / Yehoshua succession, Torah-meditation, and Jordan-preparation text tradition, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for succession after Moses, land-boundary rhetoric, courage commands, Torah meditation, officer mobilization, Transjordan obligation, and execution language against dissent.

verses.restored: It was after death of Moses, servant of YHWH, that YHWH spoke to Joshua son of Nun, Moses' attendant, saying,
Moses my servant is dead. Now rise, cross this Jordan, you and all this people, into land I am giving to them, to children of Israel.
Every place sole of your foot treads I have given to you, as I spoke to Moses.
From wilderness and this Lebanon as far as great river, river Euphrates, all land of Hittites, and as far as Great Sea toward going down of sun shall be your border.
No man shall stand before you all days of your life. As I was with Moses, I will be with you; I will not fail you and I will not forsake you.
Be strong and courageous, for you shall cause this people to inherit land I swore to their fathers to give them.
Only be very strong and courageous, keeping to do according to all Torah/instruction Moses my servant commanded you. Do not turn from it right or left, so you may act wisely wherever you go.

This scroll of Torah shall not depart from your mouth; you shall meditate/murmur in it day and night, so you may keep to do according to all written in it, for then you shall make your way prosper and then you shall act wisely.
Have I not commanded you? Be strong and courageous. Do not tremble and do not be shattered, for YHWH your Divine is with you wherever you go.
Joshua commanded officers of people, saying,

Pass through midst of camp and command people, saying, Prepare provisions for yourselves, for in three days you are crossing this Jordan to enter and possess land YHWH your Divine gives you to possess.
To Reubenites, Gadites, and half tribe of Manasseh Joshua spoke, saying,
Remember word Moses servant of YHWH commanded you, saying, YHWH your Divine gives you rest and has given you this land.
Your wives, little ones, and livestock shall dwell in land Moses gave you across Jordan, but you shall cross armed before your brothers, all mighty men of strength, and help them,

until YHWH gives rest to your brothers as to you and they also possess land YHWH your Divine gives them; then you shall return to land of your possession and possess it, which Moses servant of YHWH gave you across Jordan toward sunrise.
They answered Joshua, saying, All you command us we will do, and wherever you send us we will go.
According to all we listened to Moses, so we will listen to you. Only may YHWH your Divine be with you as he was with Moses.
Every man who rebels against your command and does not listen to your words in all you command him shall be put to death. Only be strong and courageous.

translation_notes: Joshua's authority is explicitly successor authority: he is Moses' former attendant and remains subordinate to Torah rather than replacing it.
The command to meditate or murmur Torah day and night describes embodied recitation joined to practice, not abstract positive thinking.
Courage is repeatedly connected to fidelity, communal responsibility, and leadership under instruction—not aggression or emotional invulnerability.
The expansive land boundaries are ancient promise rhetoric and must not function as modern property deeds or authorization for displacement.
Women, children, and livestock remain east of Jordan while armed men cross; their labor, vulnerability, and lack of voice should remain visible.
Verse 18 authorizes death for disobedience to Joshua and is morally dangerous. It must never justify authoritarian leadership, execution of dissenters, or cultic demands for unquestioning obedience.
safety_note: Do not use Joshua 1 to sanctify colonial expansion, militarized courage, authoritarian leadership, or execution for dissent. Center courage disciplined by Torah, shared responsibility, non-dynastic succession, and the promise of presence without turning a leader into an unquestionable ruler.
